_purpose: County Landscape Profile for Richmond County, NC. Physical geography, sensory snapshots, and landscape change narratives for the Pee Dee River gateway — railroad junction at Hamlet, Scottish settlement, cotton economy, and Camp Mackall.
display_name: Richmond County, North Carolina — Landscape Profile

physical_geography.physiographic_province: Sandhills subregion of the Atlantic Coastal Plain, transitioning to the Pee Dee River lowlands on the eastern edge. Richmond County spans from deep sandhills in the west to the broad Pee Dee River floodplain in the east.
physical_geography.elevation_range: 150–500 ft. The western Sandhills are higher and rolling; the eastern Pee Dee lowlands are flat and prone to flooding. Rockingham, the county seat, sits on moderately elevated ground between the two zones.
physical_geography.dominant_terrain: Mixed — sandy rolling hills in the west (Camp Mackall territory), agricultural lowlands along the Pee Dee in the east, and the railroad junction at Hamlet in the center. The terrain made Richmond County a crossroads between the Sandhills and the river plain.

soil_types.description: Deep, excessively drained sand on the western Sandhills. Same poor soil as Moore and Hoke counties.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Useless for row crops. The western sandhills were turpentine country and later military reservation (Camp Mackall). Settlement was sparse.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Richmond County, NC (1916; rev. 1982).

soil_types.description: Well-drained reddish-brown sandy loam in the central and eastern sections. Better agricultural soil.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Good cotton and tobacco land. The Marlboro soils along the Pee Dee corridor supported the county's plantation economy and later its sharecropping system.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Richmond County, NC (1916; rev. 1982).

soil_types.description: Rich alluvial soil along the Pee Dee River floodplain. Excellent agricultural land but subject to flooding.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: The best farmland in the county. Cotton, corn, and wheat grew on the Pee Dee bottomlands. The risk of flooding was accepted because the soil was worth it.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Richmond County, NC (1916; rev. 1982).

major_waterways.name: Pee Dee River (Great Pee Dee / Yadkin)
major_waterways.description: The Pee Dee River (formed by the Yadkin and Uwharrie rivers) flows south along Richmond County's eastern boundary. Its broad floodplain supported cotton plantations in the antebellum period. The river connected the county to markets downstream in South Carolina.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Pee Dee River gave Richmond County its eastern edge and its best farmland — {ancestor_name} likely knew the river as both opportunity and threat, the bottomland that grew the best cotton and the floodwater that could take it all in a night.

major_waterways.name: Hitchcock Creek
major_waterways.role: Central drainage, mill sites at Rockingham
major_waterways.description: A tributary of the Pee Dee flowing through Rockingham. Powered early mills and provided the water supply for the county seat.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: Hitchcock Creek ran through Rockingham — small enough to step across in summer, powerful enough to turn a millstone, and the reason the county seat grew where it did.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Longleaf pine on western sandhills, bottomland hardwood along the Pee Dee
vegetation_eras.description: The western half was classic longleaf pine savanna; the eastern half was river-bottom hardwood forest. The contrast was visible from any high point — pine to the west, broadleaf to the east, the Pee Dee floodplain green and lush where the sandhills were spare and dry.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Cotton fields on cleared Pee Dee bottomland, turpentine on western sandhills
vegetation_eras.description: Cotton drove the clearing of the Pee Dee bottomlands. Turpentine drove the exploitation of the western pine forest. The two economies coexisted within the same county, separated by soil type.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Agricultural landscape with railroad town growth and military reservation
vegetation_eras.description: Hamlet grew as a railroad junction after the Seaboard Air Line and other railroads converged there in the 1870s-1890s. Camp Mackall (1942) consumed thousands of acres of the western sandhills for airborne training. The landscape was a patchwork of farm, town, rail yard, and military reservation.
vegetation_eras.source: Lemmon, North Carolina's Role in World War II (1964).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: Hamlet in 1900 was noise, steam, and motion. The Seaboard Air Line Railroad had made this crossroads into a junction town, and trains arrived and departed around the clock. The depot was the center of everything — passengers changed trains, mail was sorted, freight was loaded and unloaded, and the lunch counter served travelers from Florida to Virginia. The steam whistle was audible from every house in town. Beyond the rail yards, cotton fields ran to the tree line, and the summer heat sat on the flat land like a weight. The smell was coal smoke from the locomotives, cottonseed from the gin, and the pervasive dust that the trains kicked up from the unpaved streets around the depot.
sensory_snapshots.source: Seaboard Air Line Railroad historical records; Richmond County Historical Collection.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: The cotton harvest along the Pee Dee in October was a landscape of labor. The white bolls stood open in fields that ran from the river bluffs to the sandy uplands, and every hand — enslaved, hired, family — was in the rows with a cotton sack. The river ran brown with autumn rain, carrying flatboats loaded with baled cotton downstream toward Cheraw and the South Carolina markets. The plantation houses along the Pee Dee were substantial frame structures with dependencies — kitchen, smokehouse, slave quarters — arrayed behind. The Scottish surnames on the plantation deeds — McRae, Leak, Steele — marked this as the southern frontier of the Cape Fear Highland settlement.
sensory_snapshots.source: Meyer, Highland Scots of North Carolina (1961); Johnson, Ante-Bellum North Carolina (1937).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: Camp Mackall in winter 1943 was mud and men. The airborne training camp sprawled across the western sandhills, barracks and tents arranged in rows on ground that had been longleaf pine forest six months earlier. Paratroopers trained in the cold rain, practicing jumps from mock towers and running obstacle courses through the scrub. The sandy soil that would not hold a crop held the barracks pins just fine. Local families who had been bought out — their sandhill farms worth almost nothing — watched from the remaining communities as sixty thousand soldiers transformed their quiet back-of-beyond into the Army's premier airborne training facility.
sensory_snapshots.source: Devlin, Gerard M. Paratrooper! The Saga of U.S. Army and Marine Parachute and Glider Combat Troops During World War II. St. Martin's Press, 1979.

landscape_changes.change: Scottish Highland settlement along the Pee Dee
landscape_changes.description: Highland Scots settled the Pee Dee corridor as part of the broader Cape Fear settlement. The area that became Richmond County was the southwestern frontier of the Argyll Colony. Scottish surnames became dominant on land deeds and church rolls.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: Scottish heritage shaped Richmond County's identity. The clan structure influenced land distribution and marriage patterns for generations after immigration.
landscape_changes.source: Meyer, Duane. The Highland Scots of North Carolina, 1732-1776. UNC Press, 1961.

landscape_changes.description: The convergence of the Seaboard Air Line and other rail lines at Hamlet created a junction town that rivaled any depot in the Sandhills. Hamlet grew from crossroads to regional transportation hub within two decades. The railroad brought commerce, travelers, and employment to a county that had been agricultural and isolated.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: For an ancestor in Richmond County after 1870, Hamlet was the window to the wider world. The railroad offered employment, market access, and the physical means of departure for families who decided to leave.
landscape_changes.source: Seaboard Air Line Railroad historical records.

landscape_changes.description: The southeastern portion of Richmond County was separated to form Scotland County in 1899, taking with it much of the old Highland settlement zone around Laurinburg. Richmond County's territory shrank but retained the Pee Dee corridor and the Hamlet junction.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: Genealogical records for ancestors in what became Scotland County before 1899 are in Richmond County courthouse. The county split is a research boundary.
landscape_changes.source: Powell, William S. The North Carolina Gazetteer. 2nd ed. UNC Press, 2010.

landscape_changes.description: Camp Mackall was built in the western sandhills of Richmond and Moore counties in 1942 as the Army's primary airborne and glider training facility. The 82nd and 101st Airborne Divisions trained here. The camp displaced farm families and consumed thousands of acres.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: Camp Mackall families were bought out on short notice in 1942. For ancestors whose land was in the camp footprint, the military acquisition was as disruptive as any migration trigger in the NEL.
landscape_changes.source: Lemmon, Sarah McCulloh. North Carolina's Role in World War II. NC Division of Archives and History, 1964.

narrative_hooks: Hamlet was where the railroads crossed — and for {ancestor_name} in {year}, the junction meant that the world beyond Richmond County was only a ticket away.

The Pee Dee River gave Richmond County its best farmland and its worst flood risk — {ancestor_name} farmed the bottomlands knowing that the river that made the soil rich could also take the crop in a single night.

Camp Mackall filled the western sandhills with paratroopers in 1942, and {ancestor_name}'s family was among those bought out — their sandhill farm valued at next to nothing by the government, their old life replaced by barracks and drop zones.

Scotland County was carved from Richmond in 1899, and the names on the new county's deeds were the same Highland Scottish surnames that had been on Richmond County deeds since the 1740s — McNeill, McLeod, McLean, McRae.

Richmond County sat between the Sandhills and the Pee Dee — and {ancestor_name} likely experienced both landscapes: the deep sand where only pine and turpentine thrived, and the river bottom where cotton actually grew.
source_references: Meyer, Duane. The Highland Scots of North Carolina, 1732-1776. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1961.
Earley, Lawrence S. Looking for Longleaf: The Fall and Rise of an American Forest. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2004.
Outland, Robert B., III. Tapping the Pines: The Naval Stores Industry in the American South. Baton Rouge: Louisiana State University Press, 2004.
Lemmon, Sarah McCulloh. North Carolina's Role in World War II. Raleigh: NC Division of Archives and History, 1964.
Powell, William S. The North Carolina Gazetteer. 2nd ed. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2010.
USDA Soil Survey of Richmond County, North Carolina. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1916; revised 1982.
